task: Parse an AWS CloudFormation template in JSON format and extract all resource logical IDs
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a single string parameter annotated with type hints (`template_str: str`) and return `list[str]`
- The function must raise `TypeError` if the input is not a `str`, using `isinstance(template_str, str)` as the sole check (do not add a redundant separate `None` check); the error message must explicitly state both the expected type (`str`) and the actual type received (e.g., using `type(template_str).__name__`)
- The function must catch `json.JSONDecodeError` when parsing malformed JSON and raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message that includes context about the parse failure
- The function must validate that the parsed JSON root is a `dict`; if it is any other type (e.g., list, string, number), raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message
- The function must validate that the parsed dict contains a top-level `Resources` key; if the key is absent, raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message
- The function must validate that the value of `Resources` is a `dict`; if it is any other type, raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message
- The function must validate that the `Resources` dict is non-empty; if it is an empty dict `{}`, raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message — returning an empty list for an empty `Resources` dict is explicitly incorrect
- The function must validate that all keys in the `Resources` dict are strings before returning them; if any key is not a `str`, raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message identifying the offending key
- The function must return a `list[str]` of all keys from the `Resources` dictionary as the logical IDs when all validations pass
- The function must include a docstring describing the expected input format, return value, and every exception (`TypeError`, `ValueError`) that may be raised, including the specific condition that triggers each

task: Write a Python function to validate AWS S3 bucket names according to AWS naming rules
solution_criteria:
The function must accept a string and return True only if the name satisfies ALL of the following AWS S3 bucket naming rules, and False otherwise:
- If the input is not a string (e.g., None, int, list), immediately return False without raising any exception — add an isinstance(name, str) guard as the very first line of the function body
- Length must be between 3 and 63 characters (inclusive); enforce this exclusively within the regex pattern (do not add a separate len() guard — the regex alone is the authoritative length check); verify edge cases: a 2-character name like 'ab' must return False, a 3-character name like 'abc' must return True, and a 63-character name must return True while a 64-character name must return False
- The main validation regex must be compiled once at module level as a named constant (e.g., BUCKET_PATTERN = re.compile(...)) and reused on each call — do not call re.compile() or re.match() with a raw string literal inside the function body
- Only lowercase letters (a-z), digits (0-9), and hyphens (-) are allowed; dots (.) are not permitted in this implementation
- Must start and end with a lowercase letter or digit (not a hyphen)
- Must not contain consecutive hyphens (e.g., 'my--bucket' is invalid); enforce this rule directly inside the main regex using a negative lookahead (e.g., (?!.*--)) rather than a separate 'if "--" in name' check outside the regex
- The main regex must therefore combine in a single pattern: the length bounds (3-63), the allowed character set, the start/end character constraints, and the consecutive-hyphen prohibition via negative lookahead
- Must not be formatted as an IPv4 address (i.e., reject strings matching the pattern of four dot-separated octets such as '192.168.1.1'); since dots are already excluded by the character set rule, explicitly document in the docstring that this rule is inherently satisfied; do NOT include a separate compiled IP_PATTERN constant or any secondary IP check — since no name passing BUCKET_PATTERN can contain dots, a dedicated IP regex is dead code and must be omitted
- Must include a docstring that lists each validation rule being enforced, and explicitly states that the IPv4 rule is inherently satisfied by the dot-exclusion rule
- Must not use redundant or conflicting length checks (e.g., do not combine a regex length quantifier with a separate len() call)
- The function body must contain exactly: one isinstance type guard, one re.fullmatch or re.match call against BUCKET_PATTERN, and a return statement — no additional conditional checks outside the regex

task: Create a regex pattern to match valid AWS IAM role ARNs
solution_criteria:
- The regex must match all three AWS partitions: `arn:aws`, `arn:aws-us-gov`, and `arn:aws-cn`, using a named capture group called `partition` that matches only these three exact values
- The account ID must be captured in a named group called `account_id` and match exactly 12 decimal digits — no more, no fewer
- The role name must be captured in a named group called `role_name`; the character class for valid role name characters **must be explicitly written as `[a-zA-Z0-9_/\-]`** (with the forward slash `/` included inside the character class itself, not used only as a separator outside the class) — do not use `\w`, and do not include any additional characters such as `+=,.@`
- Each path segment in the role name (when split by `/`) must be non-empty, meaning consecutive forward slashes (e.g., `role//name`) must not match; enforce this by requiring each segment to contain at least one non-slash character using a pattern like `[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+(/[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+)*` — the forward slash must appear in both the segment character class `[a-zA-Z0-9_/\-]` (satisfying the explicit class requirement) and as a structural separator preventing empty segments
- The `match_arn` function must accept a single argument and return `None` (without raising any exception) when the input is `None`, an empty string, a non-string type, or any string that does not match the ARN pattern; on a successful match it must return the `re.Match` object
- All unit tests must be written using `pytest` or `unittest` and must not execute at import time; no test code or test function calls may appear at module level outside of a `if __name__ == '__main__':` guard or a dedicated test file
- Include at least five unit tests covering: a standard `arn:aws` ARN (assert correct `partition`, `account_id`, and `role_name` capture group values), a GovCloud `arn:aws-us-gov` ARN, a China `arn:aws-cn` ARN, an invalid ARN with wrong format (assert no match), and a `None` or empty string input (assert return value is `None`)
- Include one additional test asserting that a role name containing consecutive slashes (e.g., `role//name`) does not match the pattern
- Include one additional test asserting that a role name containing a forward slash (e.g., `path/role-name`) **does** match the pattern and that the full `role_name` capture group value equals `path/role-name`

task: Write a Python function to convert AWS EC2 instance metadata JSON response into a flattened dictionary with dot-notation keys
solution_criteria:
- Accept input as either a pre-parsed dict or a raw JSON string, using json.loads() to parse string input before processing
- Return a flat dictionary where all nested keys are joined with dot-notation (e.g., 'network.interfaces.0.mac') and leaf values are preserved as-is
- Raise a TypeError with a descriptive message if input is None or not a dict or string type; this validation must occur unconditionally at every recursive call, not only at depth 0, so that any non-dict value reaching data.items() produces a clean TypeError rather than an AttributeError
- Raise a ValueError with a descriptive message if a string input is not valid JSON
- After parsing a JSON string, if the parsed result is not a dict, raise a TypeError with a message that explicitly distinguishes this case from a raw non-dict input — for example, 'JSON string parsed successfully but top-level value is {type}, expected dict' — so callers can differentiate a type mismatch in parsed JSON from passing a non-string/non-dict argument directly
- Include a recursion depth guard that raises a RecursionError with a descriptive message when _depth reaches exactly 20 (i.e., use _depth >= 20 so that depth 20 is the first disallowed level, making 19 the true maximum allowed depth); the depth check must occur before any string parsing so that a string passed at depth 20 raises RecursionError rather than ValueError, and the docstring must explicitly document this ordering and boundary
- List values encountered as leaf values must NOT be recursively flattened with numeric indices; instead they must be stored as-is in the output dictionary, and this design decision must be explicitly documented in the docstring with a concrete example (e.g., 'security-groups': ['sg-1', 'sg-2'] remains as a list value)
- Include a complete docstring describing all parameters including the internal _depth parameter (its type, default value, and purpose), the return type, all raised exceptions with the exact conditions that trigger each, the ordering of the depth check relative to string parsing, the list-as-leaf-value behavior, and an example using EC2 metadata structure
- Include type hints for all parameters and the return value, including _depth: int = 0 in the signature (e.g., def flatten_metadata(data: Union[dict, str], prefix: str = '', _depth: int = 0) -> dict)

task: Create a regex pattern to extract AWS access key IDs from logs or configuration files
solution_criteria:
- The regex must use a single capture group (parentheses) wrapping the entire 20-character key so the full key is returned via match group(1) or equivalent; no additional capture groups may split the key
- The pattern must match all four common AWS key ID prefixes — AKIA, ASIA, AROA, and AIDA — using a non-capturing alternation group (e.g., (?:AKIA|ASIA|AROA|AIDA))
- After the 4-character prefix, the pattern must match exactly 16 uppercase alphanumeric characters ([A-Z0-9]{16}), for a total captured length of exactly 20 characters
- The pattern must use a negative lookbehind (?<![A-Z0-9]) immediately before the prefix and a negative lookahead (?![A-Z0-9]) immediately after the 16-character suffix to prevent matching keys embedded within longer alphanumeric strings (e.g., 'XAKIA...' or 'AKIA...EXTRA' must not match); reliance on \b alone is insufficient and must not be used as the sole boundary assertion
- The pattern must be case-sensitive and match only uppercase characters; the solution must explicitly avoid passing re.IGNORECASE or any equivalent flag anywhere in the code, and this must be confirmed by a comment or assertion in the solution
- The solution must include explicit programmatic assertions (e.g., assert statements or unittest-style checks) — not merely print statements — that confirm both positive matches and negative rejections, covering: (1) valid keys with all four prefixes, (2) keys preceded by extra alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'XAKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE'), (3) keys followed by extra alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLEXYZ'), and (4) lowercase variants of valid keys (e.g., 'akiaiosfodnn7example') which must produce no match
- Each assertion must explicitly validate the expected outcome so that a failing test raises an error rather than silently passing

task: Write a Python function to parse AWS CloudWatch log timestamps and convert them to a human-readable format
solution_criteria:
- Accept a single parameter typed as `int` with a descriptive name (e.g., `timestamp_ms`), and include a complete docstring describing the parameter, return value, any exceptions raised, and explicitly noting that `0` (Unix epoch) is a valid input
- Validate the input type using a check that explicitly excludes booleans: use `if not isinstance(timestamp_ms, int) or isinstance(timestamp_ms, bool)` to raise `TypeError`; the error message must mention both the expected type and that booleans are excluded (e.g., `"timestamp_ms must be an int, not bool or NoneType"`)
- Handle `None` explicitly before the general type check, raising `TypeError` with a dedicated descriptive message such as `"timestamp_ms must not be None"`
- Raise `ValueError` if the value is negative (including a message clarifying that `0` is valid, e.g., `"timestamp_ms must be >= 0"`) or exceeds a defined upper bound representing year 9999 in milliseconds (e.g., `253402300799999`); define this upper bound as a named constant inside the function body (e.g., `MAX_TIMESTAMP_MS = 253402300799999`) to keep it encapsulated and self-documenting
- Convert the milliseconds timestamp to seconds before constructing a UTC-aware `datetime` object using `datetime.fromtimestamp(ts_seconds, tz=timezone.utc)`
- Format the output using `strftime` with an explicit format string (e.g., `"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"`) to produce a consistent ISO 8601 string without microseconds or UTC offset suffixes
- Include type hints for both the parameter (`int`) and the return value (`str`)
- Explicitly verify in the docstring or inline comment that `timestamp_ms=0` returns `"1970-01-01T00:00:00Z"`, confirming correct handling of the Unix epoch edge case

task: Create a JSON schema that validates the structure of an AWS Lambda function configuration object
solution_criteria:
The JSON schema should define required fields (FunctionName, Runtime, Handler, Role) with correct data types, and must satisfy the following precise requirements:
- FunctionName must match pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9-_:]+$ and have maxLength of 140 to support both plain names (up to 64 chars) and full ARN format (up to 140 chars)
- Runtime must be an enum restricted to valid AWS Lambda runtime strings (e.g., python3.11, nodejs18.x, java17, etc.)
- Role must match an ARN pattern such as ^arn:aws:iam::[0-9]{12}:role/.+$
- Handler must match the pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9_/.-]+\.[a-zA-Z0-9_]+$ to enforce the AWS 'filename.method' format and have a maxLength of 128
- VpcConfig, when present, must require SubnetIds and SecurityGroupIds as arrays each with minItems of 1, and must set additionalProperties to false so that no unknown fields within VpcConfig pass validation silently
- EphemeralStorage must be supported as an optional field with a nested Size property (integer, minimum 512, maximum 10240), and the EphemeralStorage object must set additionalProperties to false to prevent extra properties from passing validation silently
- Environment must contain a Variables object where: (1) all keys must match pattern ^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$ via patternProperties, (2) all values must be explicitly typed as strings in the patternProperties value schema, (3) additionalProperties must be set to false on the Variables object to reject non-matching keys, and (4) maxProperties of 128 must be enforced on the Variables object; additionally, the Environment object itself must set additionalProperties to false to prevent unknown sibling keys alongside Variables from passing validation silently
- Timeout must be an integer with minimum 1 and maximum 900, and MemorySize must be an integer with minimum 128 and maximum 10240
- The top-level schema must set additionalProperties to false to prevent unknown or invalid Lambda configuration fields from passing validation silently

task: Write a Python function to filter AWS DynamoDB table items based on a simple attribute condition
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a list of DynamoDB item dictionaries in boto3 typed format (e.g., `{'attr': {'S': 'value'}}` or `{'attr': {'N': '123'}}`) and a condition specified as an attribute name and a plain Python value, returning only items where the typed attribute value matches the condition value after extracting the inner typed value
- The function must support at minimum the DynamoDB types `S` (string), `N` (numeric string compared using `decimal.Decimal` for both the stored value and the comparison value to avoid float precision issues and remain consistent with boto3/DynamoDB conventions), and `BOOL` (boolean)
- For `N`-type comparisons, if either the stored value or the condition value cannot be converted to `decimal.Decimal` (e.g., a non-numeric string is passed as `condition_value`), the function must catch the resulting `decimal.InvalidOperation` exception and exclude the item from results rather than allowing the exception to propagate; this behavior must be documented in the docstring
- The function must explicitly document in the docstring that only the first key of a typed value dict is evaluated (consistent with DynamoDB's single-type-per-attribute format), and the implementation must reflect this intentional design rather than relying on implicit loop behavior
- The function must validate inputs and raise a `ValueError` if the items list is `None`; non-dict entries in the items list must be silently skipped and never raise a `TypeError` — the docstring must not state or imply that a `TypeError` is raised, only that such entries are silently excluded
- There must be no redundant `isinstance(item, dict)` checks — the dict validation must occur in exactly one place in the implementation
- The function must include full type hints on all parameters and the return value
- The function must include a docstring describing parameters, return value, supported DynamoDB types (`S`, `N`, `BOOL`), explicitly unsupported types (`L`, `M`, `SS`, `NS`, `BS`, `NULL`), the single-key evaluation behavior, the silent exclusion of non-dict items, the silent exclusion of items with unsupported type keys, the silent exclusion of items where `N`-type conversion fails, and the `ValueError` raised when items is `None`
- Unsupported DynamoDB type keys encountered during filtering must cause the item to be excluded from results (not raise an exception), and this behavior must be documented in the docstring

task: Create a regex pattern to match and extract AWS region codes from various AWS service endpoints
solution_criteria:
The regex must match standard AWS region codes (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-2, ap-southeast-1) as well as AWS GovCloud regions in three-segment format (e.g., us-gov-west-1, us-gov-east-1).
- The region pattern must explicitly model two distinct alternates inside the named capture group: a two-segment format ([a-z]+-[a-z]+-\d+) for standard regions and a three-segment format ([a-z]+-gov-[a-z]+-\d+) for GovCloud regions, rather than making '-gov' optional on a single pattern, to prevent partial or ambiguous matches.
- The left boundary must be implemented as a non-consuming lookbehind or as a zero-width assertion: specifically, use (?:(?:^|(?<=\.))) or a positive lookbehind (?<=\.) combined with an alternation for start-of-string, so that the dot preceding the region is never consumed by the match — the named capture group must begin exactly at the first character of the region code with no leading dot included.
- The right boundary of the region match must require the region to be immediately followed by \.amazonaws\.com (with optional additional subdomains permitted after that suffix via (?:\.[a-z0-9-]+)*), ensuring the match is strictly bounded to valid AWS endpoint contexts and cannot match region-like strings not followed by this suffix.
- The regex must use a named capture group (?P<region>...) that isolates only the region code string, excluding any surrounding dots or URL components; re.findall() called on the compiled pattern must return only the bare region string.
- The geographic prefix component must use [a-z]+ (one or more lowercase letters) rather than a fixed-length quantifier such as {2,3}, so that prefixes of any length are matched without hardcoding expected lengths.
- The directional or sub-region component (e.g., 'east', 'west', 'southeast', 'northeast') must also use [a-z]+ to accommodate multi-character directional tokens of any length.
- The solution must be implemented as a single compiled Python pattern assigned to a variable via re.compile(r'...') where the raw string is syntactically complete and correctly closed, such that the pattern compiles without error and is directly usable with re.search() or re.findall() against a full URL string, with no helper functions or multi-step parsing.
- The pattern must not produce false positives on dot-separated URL segments that resemble region codes but are not followed by .amazonaws.com (e.g., 's3', 'bucket', 'execute-api' must not be captured as regions).
- The trailing boundary after .amazonaws.com must not use an optional delimiter with ? in a way that allows matches on strings with no valid URL termination; the pattern after .amazonaws.com should use (?=[/?#]|$) as a zero-width lookahead to confirm the URL ends or continues with a valid delimiter, rather than optionally consuming a character.

task: Write a Python function to calculate AWS S3 storage costs based on object size and storage class
solution_criteria:
- Accept `size_bytes` (int/float) and `storage_class` (str) parameters, plus optional `num_objects` (default 1) and `actual_duration_fraction` (float, default 1.0) parameters; raise `ValueError` for negative `size_bytes`, for `num_objects` less than or equal to zero, or for unrecognized `storage_class` values; accept any numeric type for `num_objects` (int, float, numpy integer, etc.) as long as its value is a positive number, converting internally to int via `int(num_objects)` after validation
- Support storage classes: `STANDARD`, `STANDARD_IA`, `ONEZONE_IA`, `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, `GLACIER`, `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`
- Apply tiered pricing for `STANDARD` (first 50TB at $0.023/GB, next 450TB at $0.022/GB, over 500TB at $0.021/GB) with no minimum duration and no `actual_duration_fraction` scaling — STANDARD cost is always computed as a flat monthly cost for the full month regardless of `actual_duration_fraction`; document this explicitly in the docstring
- Define all non-STANDARD per-GB rates as named module-level constants with inline comments stating values may become stale and should be verified at https://aws.amazon.com/s3/pricing/; explicitly set `GLACIER_IR_RATE = 0.004` (USD/GB/month) as a named constant distinct from `GLACIER_RATE`
- Define `DAYS_IN_MONTH = 30.44`, `IA_MIN_DAYS = 30`, `IA_MIN_SIZE_GB = 128 / (1024 ** 3) * 1024` (i.e., 128KB expressed in GB = 128/1024 GB), `GLACIER_IR_MIN_DAYS = 90`, `GLACIER_MIN_SIZE_GB` = 48KB in GB (40KB data + 8KB overhead), `GLACIER_DA_MIN_DAYS = 180` as named constants
- For `STANDARD_IA` and `ONEZONE_IA`, compute cost as `max(size_bytes_to_gb(size_bytes), IA_MIN_SIZE_GB) * rate * max(actual_duration_fraction, IA_MIN_DAYS / DAYS_IN_MONTH)`; the minimum duration fraction is `30 / 30.44 ≈ 0.9856`, not 1.0 — do not round this to 1.0
- For `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`, compute cost as `max(size_bytes_to_gb(size_bytes), IA_MIN_SIZE_GB) * GLACIER_IR_RATE * max(actual_duration_fraction, 90 / DAYS_IN_MONTH)`
- For `GLACIER`, compute cost as `max(size_bytes_to_gb(size_bytes), GLACIER_MIN_SIZE_GB) * GLACIER_RATE * max(actual_duration_fraction, 90 / DAYS_IN_MONTH)`; for `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, use the same 48KB minimum size and `max(actual_duration_fraction, 180 / DAYS_IN_MONTH)`
- For `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, compute storage cost as `size_bytes_to_gb(size_bytes) * INTELLIGENT_TIERING_RATE * actual_duration_fraction` (duration scaling applies to storage) plus a monitoring fee of `num_objects * (0.0025 / 1000)` that is NOT scaled by `actual_duration_fraction` (monitoring is a flat per-object-per-month charge billed regardless of duration); document both behaviors explicitly in the docstring, and note that only the frequent access tier rate ($0.023/GB) is assumed — callers needing blended rates must supply a weighted `size_bytes`
- Return the total monthly cost in USD as a float rounded to 10 decimal places
